title: Chapter 40: Alone Again
content: The vending machine's screen flickered. Wren pressed her palm against the glass and pushed. Electricity arced through the circuitry — a controlled surge, delicate as a scalpel — and the locking mechanism clicked open. She pulled the door wide and took what she needed. Protein bars. Bottled water. A bag of chips that was six months past expiration and tasted like cardboard and salt and freedom.

She ate sitting on the floor of an office building's server closet, back against a rack of humming drives, the sound of a hundred processors thinking their electronic thoughts covering the sound of her chewing. The building was closed for the night. Security systems looped on a feed she'd glitched hours ago — a perfect sixty-second loop of empty corridors that would hold until dawn.
She had maybe eight hours.

It had been four days since Ash was taken. Four days of moving through the city like smoke — surfacing, scavenging, vanishing. No community. No safe houses. No one who knew she existed except the machines that hunted her and the corporation that had made her.
Four days alone.

The first day she'd spent in shock. Running on autopilot — glitch, move, hide, repeat. The grief sat in her chest like a stone and she couldn't feel around it, couldn't think past it. She'd found a condemned building and crawled into a corner and stayed there for sixteen hours, barely breathing.

The second day, the rage came. Not the cold strategic anger of planning — this was animal, feral, a heat that started in her gut and spread until her skin felt too tight. She'd stood in an alley and glitched every camera within a hundred meters. All of them. Simultaneously. Not a fuzz or a static overlay — she'd *fried* them, overloaded their circuits until the glass cracked and smoke curled from the housings.
Twenty-seven cameras. Dead. She'd never pushed that hard before. Never reached that far.
She'd collapsed after. Woken up on wet asphalt with blood trickling from her nose and no memory of how she'd gotten there.
But she remembered the feeling. That moment when the power surged through her and every camera died and for one blazing second she wasn't invisible — she was *unstoppable*.

That was the gift NeuroScale had given her. Stage 4. Unprecedented. The same classification they'd given Ash before he tore himself free of their machines and vanished into the city's underbelly for three years.

He'd learned what he was alone. She didn't have that luxury — or that curse. She had files to publish and a promise to keep, and she couldn't do either of those things if she burned out chasing revenge.
So on the third day, she started practicing.

The abandoned subway platform was perfect. No working cameras. No networked systems. No one to see a woman standing in the dark, raising her hand toward a dead monitor mounted on the pillar, and *willing* it on.

The screen flickered. White noise. Then: a face. Hannah Marsh. Nineteen years old. The photograph from her file, the intake photo with the hollow cheeks and the eyes that had already given up. Wren held the image for five seconds. Ten. Fifteen. The monitor screamed with static and the photograph held, pixel-perfect, every detail preserved.
She let go. The screen went dark.
Her nose bled. Her hands shook. But she'd done it. She'd overridden a dead display with nothing but the electricity in her own nervous system and the image in her own mind.

She did it again. And again. Different faces — Marcus Fell, Devi Chandra, Sofia Reyes. Different screens — a broken departure board, a shattered advertising display, the cracked monitor of a ticket kiosk. Each time she pushed, the power came easier. Each time she released, the recovery came faster.
She wasn't just glitching anymore. She was *writing*. Imposing her will on the city's electronic infrastructure. Making machines show what she wanted them to show.
She was becoming a ghost in the machine. Literally.
On the fourth day she found the data center.

It was beneath a corporate high-rise — forty floors of glass and steel that had been vacated after a merger, the building sitting empty while lawyers argued over assets. The data center in the basement was still running. Still connected. Racks upon racks of servers humming in climate-controlled darkness, their status lights blinking green and amber, their hard drives spinning with the digital memory of a city that never slept.

Wren stood in the aisle between the racks and listened. The hum was everywhere — a deep subsonic vibration that she felt in her fillings, in her bones, in the glitch that lived behind her navel. The machines weren't just making noise. They were *calling*. Every server, every switch, every fiber optic cable threaded through the walls — she could feel them. The city's digital backbone, right here, right under her hands.

She reached out and touched a rack. The metal was cool against her palm. She closed her eyes and pushed — not hard, not yet, just a whisper of connection — and the server hummed back at her. Acknowledged. *I see you.*
She opened her eyes. The status lights on the rack had changed. All green. All hers.

She had access. To the feeds. To the networks. To every camera and screen and broadcast system in the city, if she pushed hard enough. All she needed was the signal — and she had the Signal Key in her pack, the device that Silas had died protecting, the key that could unlock NeuroScale's encrypted channels and expose every secret they'd buried.

She set up camp. The server room had backup power that would last months. The climate control kept it warm. The hum of the machines masked her presence from any heat-tracking system that tried to scan through the building's shell. It was the safest place she'd found since the Underlayer.
Safer, maybe. Because this time, she was the one watching.

She found a marker in a supply drawer — a thick black Sharpie, half-dried. She started drawing on the server room walls. Plans. Maps. The layout of NeuroScale's facilities, reconstructed from the files on the drive. Ash's containment record — Facility West, Sublevel 3, Subject 11. The Signal Key's architecture, sketched from memory and guesswork. Timelines. Strategies. contingencies.
The walls filled up. A web of lines and words and arrows that spiraled outward from a single point: ASH.

She circled his name. Drew an arrow to the broadcast tower across the city — the one that handled the primary data feeds for three major networks. If she could get the Signal Key to that tower, she could push the files into every connected device in Meridian. Phones. Tablets. Billboards. Car dashboards. Every screen would show the same thing: the names, the records, the proof. NeuroScale's crimes made visible in a way that couldn't be erased or explained away.
But pushing a broadcast that massive would cost her. Maybe everything. The same way Devi Chandra's stress test cost her. The same way every overreach cost something.

Wren looked at her hands. The dried blood around her cuticles. The faint tremor that never quite went away anymore. She was changing. She could feel it — not just the power growing, but something structural shifting inside her. The glitch was rewriting her, neuron by neuron, connection by connection, turning her into something that was half human and half signal.
She didn't know what she was becoming.
But she knew what she had to do.
She capped the marker. Looked at the walls — at the plans and the names and the arrow pointing at the broadcast tower. At Ash's name circled in the center of it all.
*Publish the files. Don't come for me.*

The first instruction, she would follow. The files would be published. Every name. Every crime. Every lie NeuroScale had told to every subject who'd walked through their doors looking for help and found a machine instead.
The second instruction —
She picked up the Signal Key. Turned it over in her hands. The metal was warm, as if it remembered the hands that had carried it before her.
"Don't come for me," she whispered. Ash's voice in her memory. Ash's breath, failing. Ash's gaze, the last thing he'd given her before the darkness took him.
She set the Key down.
Then she picked it up again.
I'll publish the files, she thought. I'll make them see us — all of us, every subject, every name. And then I'll come for you.
Not because you asked me not to.
Because you asked me to see you. And I do.
She turned back to the servers. Reached out. Let the hum fill her.
The city was watching.
It was about to see something it had never seen before.
